concern.text: I do not currently have usable codebase access, so I cannot do the required concrete searches. The local command runner fails before execution with `bwrap: Unknown option --argv0`, which prevents me from reading `.architecture/INDEX.md`, searching legacy folders, grepping for existing adversary/gauntlet code, or checking dependency manifests.

The ownership model is muddled and that usually means nobody actually owns the failure. You say guardrails are “run by Claude inline during debate” and not part of the pipeline, but you also require mandatory behavior, artifact awareness, manifest access, and phase-doc integration. That is not a design, that is wishful glue. If the whole thing depends on Claude remembering to do conversational steps correctly, you did not “enforce” anything.

“Dynamic prompts” are being sold as a fix, but the spec never proves prompt variability is the root cause of the misses. Your own evidence says the pipeline is blind to editorial regressions and taxonomy/synthesis dropped concerns. That points to synthesis and process failures, not “the security prompt needs scope-aware wording.” This redesign may just add prompt theater while the real failure mode stays intact.

User review is a bottleneck disguised as safety. Section 1.4 makes Claude present all generated prompts for review, edit, skip, regenerate. On a real workflow this is tedious enough that people will smash “approve all” or skip the whole exercise mentally. So you added friction, not quality. The spec never explains why users are qualified to review adversary prompts better than the system that generated them.

The fallback behavior is self-defeating. Hash mismatch on `approved-prompts.json` falls back to static personas and full default roster. That means the system silently downgrades from the “new safer smarter” mode to the old mode exactly when the spec changed and review is most needed. A stale prompt artifact should trigger regeneration, not a warning and degradation. This is a design hole, not error handling.

The artifact model is brittle. You persist approved prompts keyed by a 12-char truncated SHA-256 of raw spec contents and assume that matches the existing convention. Great, so whitespace changes, reordered headings, or editorial cleanup all invalidate the artifact and dump you back to static fallback. You are tying behavioral configuration to raw file bytes with zero normalization and pretending that is robustness.

The “skip adversaries by scope” feature is a coverage footgun. You say skipping is user-controlled, not automatic, but you also recommend skips like “local-only CLI tool: PARA, FLOW.” That’s exactly how teams convince themselves no security or data-flow review is needed right before they add file parsing, shelling out, plugin hooks, or telemetry. “Local-only” software still has attack surfaces. Your recommendation table is overconfident and will be misused.

The scope classifier is underspecified and dangerously fake-precise. You define `exposure`, `domain`, `risk_signals`, and `stack` as if those are obvious, but real specs will span multiple domains, multiple exposure levels, and partial stacks. What happens for “internal API now, public later,” “CLI plus daemon,” or “library with network side effects”? You force a tidy JSON shape onto messy inputs and then drive prompt selection off it. Bad classification means bad prompts, and the spec has no mitigation beyond “user reviews it.”

The spec contradicts itself on enforcement. Section 3 says taxonomy must be enforced “in code, not just in phase docs,” but then Section 3.2 says `synthesis_extract.py` does not assign categories and Section 3.3 just prepends a prompt header telling Claude to use exact categories. That is not code enforcement. That is another prompt. You are claiming determinism and delivering vibes.

The split between `ADVERSARIES`, `ADVERSARY_TEMPLATES`, and `GUARDRAILS` is registry sprawl. You now have static runtime adversaries, template adversaries, aliases, and separate guardrails using the old dataclass. That is four conceptual lookup systems for one feature family. This will drift. Someone updates focus areas in the template and forgets the static fallback persona. Someone adds a new adversary to one registry but not another. You created sync debt by design.

The taxonomy itself is mushy. Categories like “Correctness Bugs,” “Race Conditions,” “Failure Modes,” “Operability,” and “Underspecification” overlap badly. Your own coverage matrix admits secondaries all over the place. A concern about retry storms with stale cache invalidation could plausibly be Race Conditions, Failure Modes, Scalability, or Operability depending on mood. If categories are that squishy, forcing exactly one primary category just creates arbitrary synthesis noise.

The synthesis extraction format throws away too much information. One line per concern with first 150 chars of text is a compression hack, not a reliable synthesis substrate. If the real differentiator is later in the concern text, the one-pass synthesis will misclassify or merge wrong. You are trying to fix missed concerns by further lossy summarization. Brilliant.

Sorting synthesis input by concern ID is deterministic but semantically stupid. IDs reflect adversary prefix and hash, not importance, dependency, severity, or topic. So the input order is stable but arbitrary. If order influences the synthesis model, you are anchoring it on nonsense.

The guardrail model is way too optimistic about “cheap checks.” `consistency_auditor`, `scope_creep_detector`, and `requirements_tracer` are not simple validators; they require cross-document semantic reasoning over changing specs, user stories, roadmap manifests, and prior approvals. Cheap models are exactly how you get fake confidence and missed contradictions. You are assigning the most editorially subtle work to the weakest inference tier.

The spec handwaves the hardest input problem for guardrails: what exactly is “the original problem statement / requirements” and what counts as “explicitly approved” scope additions? Section 4.1 says SCOPE needs session context and TRACE needs roadmap manifest, but there is no source-of-truth schema, no approval log format, no canonical extraction process. So these checks depend on conversational memory and ad hoc summaries. That is unreliable garbage.

“Substantive revision” is undefined operationally. Section 4.2 says guardrails run after every substantive spec revision, not typo fixes or formatting. Fine. Who decides? Claude? The user? A diff heuristic? If Claude guesses wrong, contradictions slip through. If Claude guesses conservatively, token cost explodes and workflow slows to a crawl. You left the trigger as a vibes-based judgment call.

The blocking policy is inconsistent and weak. You say guardrails are mandatory and findings must be resolved before the next round, then immediately say the user can always say “proceed anyway,” and SCOPE/TRACE failures are soft stops. So these are not guardrails, they are suggestions with ceremony. The system still allows accumulation of the exact regressions it claims to prevent.

The roster optimization logic is weak and partly self-contradictory. You merge PREV and LAZY because they overlap, but refuse to merge PEDA and ASSH despite admitting category overlap because “hostile tone produces different findings.” So overlap matters except when you like the vibe. That is not principled design, that is cargo culting one lucky adversary.

`traffic_engineer` is under-scoped relative to the claimed gap. You say the gap is throughput, fan-out, concurrency limits, thundering herds, then define valid dismissal around rate limiting, backpressure, bounded queues. That ignores data skew, write amplification, storage IOPS ceilings, partition rebalancing, lock contention, async task pileups, and downstream SLO coupling. The adversary is narrower than the problem statement.

The recovery ownership change is shallow. You say `burned_oncall` now “owns” degraded mode, failover/failback, retry loops, partial recovery correctness. Fine, but FLOW still covers failure propagation and underspecification around data movement and dependencies. Without sharper boundary rules, both will keep surfacing adjacent concerns and you’ll still have synthesis overlap. “Explicit ownership” in prose does not resolve overlap in model behavior.

The migration story around IDs is sloppy. You keep `LAZY` and `PREV` for historical lookups, introduce `MINI` for new runs, and rely on alias resolution. That means historical analytics by adversary family become muddied unless every downstream report understands lineage. The spec says “no migration needed,” which is lazy nonsense. No data migration maybe, but definitely reporting and interpretation migration.

The spec invents “Gauntlet Fix Interaction Validation” as a manual reasoning ritual with no concrete mechanism. “Identify which other sections/behaviors the fix interacts with” is not actionable engineering; it is a platitude. There is no check format, no artifact, no decision rule, no required evidence. This will degrade into Claude saying “looks fine” and moving on.

Unknown adversaries being skipped with a warning is a bad default. If roster configuration is wrong, silently reducing coverage is exactly the kind of partial failure that poisons trust. The spec should fail closed for unknown required adversaries, not shrug and continue.

The error-handling policy is inconsistent about crash tolerance. Corrupt JSON is “hard crash with ValueError, never silently ignore,” invalid run log for synthesis is exit 2, missing prompts is warning and fallback, unknown adversary name is skip with warning. These are wildly different resilience strategies for closely related configuration problems. There is no stated principle, so operators will get arbitrary behavior.

The spec claims “no changes to existing pipeline code” for guardrails, but it absolutely changes the operational pipeline. Debate now includes mandatory checkpoint checks, finalize gets CONS, gauntlet incorporation requires interaction validation. Saying this is not a phase change because `debate.py` stays untouched is bureaucratic wordplay. User-visible workflow changed materially.

The testing strategy misses the failure you actually care about: semantic regressions across revisions. You have unit tests for alias resolution and exact output format, but nothing rigorous for the guardrail checks beyond registration. The whole redesign is about catching contradictions introduced during incorporation, yet there is no concrete regression corpus or golden-case test plan for CONS/SCOPE/TRACE behavior.

The “zero adversaries remain after filtering” hard stop is correct, but it exposes that the skip/review model can trivialize the gauntlet. If a user can skip down to one or two adversaries, the process still technically runs but coverage collapses. The spec has no minimum viable roster or warning threshold besides zero, which is absurdly low.

Section 1.3 says Claude writes approved prompts to `.adversarial-spec-gauntlet/approved-prompts.json`, but the broader system is explicitly conversational and not a standalone CLI. So now a conversation agent is expected to create and manage a runtime artifact the pipeline depends on. There is no locking, no overwrite policy, no concurrency story, no multi-spec workspace isolation beyond hash. This will bite as soon as more than one spec is active.

The division between “gauntlet adversaries” and “guardrails” is arbitrary enough to create blind spots. Editorial regressions can encode architectural changes, and architectural fixes can introduce editorial contradictions. You split by cost and timing, not by actual problem separability. That means bugs at the boundary will ping-pong between tiers or get missed by both.

The whole redesign increases complexity faster than it increases certainty. New template type, new registry, new artifact, new classifier, new skip logic, new guardrail tier, new extraction script, new roster member, new aliasing rules, new finalize step. And after all that, the core enforcement still depends on Claude reading docs, remembering context, and doing the right thing in conversation. That’s a lot of machinery for something still held together by “please be careful this time.”

The prompt generation examples encourage external knowledge lookup inside persona text, like “look up OWASP top 10 for this stack.” That is an execution-time capability assumption, not a persona trait. If the runtime model has no browsing or inconsistent tool access, the prompt becomes bogus. You are embedding tool assumptions into reviewer prompts without stating execution guarantees.

**Unverified assumption that Claude can present interactive approve/adjust UI controls.** Claim: Section 1.2 and 1.4 show UI controls like `[Confirm] [Adjust]`, `[Approve all] [Edit specific] [Skip specific] [Regenerate all]`, and Section 4.3 shows `[Fix CONS findings] [Approve/remove SCOPE additions] [Proceed to R4]`. Why this needs verification: This reads like an actual UI contract, but the spec also says "Claude handles this conversationally." The alternative is that Claude Code only supports plain text conversation, meaning these controls are fictional and the implementation guidance is misleading. Evidence required: Official Claude Code interaction docs or an existing skill in this repo that already renders actionable buttons in this exact form. Otherwise this should be rewritten as conversational text only. Dependency risk: Dynamic prompt review and checkpoint-guardrail UX both assume this interaction model.

**Fallback behavior on spec-hash mismatch may silently disable the redesign.** Claim: Section 1.6/1.7 says hash mismatch only warns to stderr and falls back to static personas and full roster. Why this needs verification: The spec assumes stderr warnings are visible and actionable in the Claude-driven workflow. The alternative is that users never see the warning, so they think dynamic prompts ran when they did not. Evidence required: Proof from the current execution environment that stderr warnings surface clearly to the user, or a prototype showing the UX. Dependency risk: This affects trust in the system; stale prompt files could silently cause old behavior.

**Unverified external model-cost assumption in checkpoint guardrails.** Claim: Section 4.2 says guardrails should "Use a cheap/free model (Gemini Flash, or Claude inline if spec fits in context)" and estimates "~20-80K input tokens per checkpoint." Why this needs verification: This assumes those models are actually available in the target Claude Code workflow, that they can be selected reliably per call, and that their context windows and pricing make this cheap enough. The alternative is that Claude Code cannot deterministically route to Gemini Flash here, or that context/pricing/latency makes per-round guardrails operationally expensive. Evidence required: Official Claude Code / provider-routing docs, or an implemented prototype showing model selection, token counts, latency, and cost on a real debate session. Dependency risk: The entire "run after every substantive revision" workflow depends on this being cheap and available.

**Unverified assumption that `.adversarial-spec-gauntlet/approved-prompts.json` is the right persistence mechanism for a Claude skill.** Claim: Section 1.6 requires Claude to write approved prompts to `.adversarial-spec-gauntlet/approved-prompts.json` and later `run_gauntlet()` loads it. Why this needs verification: The spec assumes the conversational agent can and should persist state to a workspace artifact that later pipeline code can trust. The alternative is that Claude sessions are ephemeral, multiple concurrent sessions can race, or the working directory during skill execution is not stable. Evidence required: A prototype end-to-end run proving Claude-created artifact persistence is reliable across skill steps and sessions, plus docs on current working directory and artifact conventions for Claude Code skills. Dependency risk: The whole dynamic-prompt mechanism fails if this artifact is not reliably produced and consumed.

**Spec hash design is asserted to "match existing convention" without citation.** Claim: Section 1.6 says truncated SHA-256 "Matches the existing `spec_hash` convention used in gauntlet checkpoint filenames." Why this needs verification: This is exactly the kind of repo-internal system claim that needs proof. The alternative is that existing code uses a different hash function, different truncation length, normalized content instead of raw file bytes, or includes file path/timestamp. Evidence required: Citation to the actual code path implementing current `spec_hash`, or a quoted snippet from the existing checkpoint filename generator. Dependency risk: Hash mismatch handling, artifact reuse, and compatibility claims all depend on this.

**"No changes to downstream phases" is an unproven assumption.** Claim: Section 1.7: "No changes to: system prompt template construction, model dispatch, concern parsing, or any downstream phases." Why this needs verification: The redesign changes prompt generation, roster composition, and synthesis input shape. The alternative is that subtle downstream behavior depends on prompt structure or roster identity. Evidence required: Code-level review or prototype run demonstrating unchanged downstream outputs except intended differences. Dependency risk: This assumption cascades into underestimated implementation and testing scope.

**The spec assumes adversary skipping is safe because skipped adversaries are removed before `generate_attacks()`, but does not verify downstream phases tolerate roster changes.** Claim: Section 1.7 says removing skipped adversaries from the list is sufficient and no downstream phases need changes. Why this needs verification: This assumes evaluation, metrics, reporting, and synthesis do not expect a fixed roster. The alternative is that downstream code expects all canonical adversaries to appear, or computes coverage/acceptance stats by known roster membership. Evidence required: Citation from downstream phase code/tests showing variable roster sizes and missing adversaries are already supported. Dependency risk: A core premise of user-driven adversary skipping rests on this.

**No proof that `run_gauntlet()` is actually the single integration point described.** Claim: Section 1.7 says "`run_gauntlet()` in `orchestrator.py` is the integration point. It owns" loading prompts, validating hash, filtering adversaries, and passing prompts. Why this needs verification: This is a codebase-structure claim. The alternative is that gauntlet runs can enter through other paths, direct `generate_attacks()` calls, or tests/scripts bypassing `run_gauntlet()`. Evidence required: Citation to current call graph or existing orchestrator entrypoints showing all gauntlet runs flow through `run_gauntlet()`. Dependency risk: If any path bypasses this function, skipped adversaries or prompt overrides will not be enforced consistently.

**Merging PREV and LAZY into `minimalist` is justified by one gauntlet’s accepted-concern count, not by demonstrated behavioral equivalence.** Claim: Section 2.1 cites "Combined 0/23 accepted concerns" and concludes they should merge. Why this needs verification: Low acceptance is not proof of redundancy; it may reflect bad prompts, synthesis bias, or evaluator blind spots. The alternative is that each adversary finds different low-frequency issues that were missed in one dataset. Evidence required: Side-by-side analysis of raw findings across multiple runs, showing overlap in concerns and low unique yield, not just accepted-count overlap. Dependency risk: If the merge premise is false, the redesign removes useful diversity.

**Coverage-matrix claim "Every category now has a primary adversary. No gaps." is unsupported.** Claim: Section 2.6 explicitly declares no gaps after adding `traffic_engineer` and merging others. Why this needs verification: This is an empirical claim about detection coverage, but no evidence is provided beyond two failure reports from one project. The alternative is that important categories remain weak, especially editorial consistency, privacy/compliance, or dependency/supply-chain review. Evidence required: Citation from the failure reports mapping missed issues to these exact taxonomy categories, or benchmark runs across multiple specs showing improved category coverage. Dependency risk: The roster redesign justification depends heavily on this statement.

**The spec assumes ASSH's tone reliably induces a different reasoning path.** Claim: Section 1 and 2.3 say the hostile tone "forces the model into a different reasoning path" and therefore should be preserved. Why this needs verification: This is a model-behavior claim. The alternative is that tone effects are inconsistent across models, releases, or safety layers, making this benefit non-portable. Evidence required: Experimental results across the actual models used in the gauntlet, with measurable delta in issue discovery attributable to tone. Dependency risk: Prompt-template design preserves tone as a first-class invariant on the basis of this assumption.

**The spec assumes Claude can classify scope "inline" with sufficient consistency to drive runtime behavior.** Claim: Section 1.2 says no separate LLM call is needed because Claude is already reading the spec. Why this needs verification: This assumes consistent, auditable classification in a conversational step. The alternative is that different sessions produce different classifications for the same spec, changing prompts and roster behavior unpredictably. Evidence required: Repeated-run prototype showing low variance in classification for the same input, or explicit deterministic rules instead of freeform classification. Dependency risk: Prompt selection, skip recommendations, and stored prompt artifacts all depend on this.

**Scope classification schema is invented without evidence that it is sufficient or stable.** Claim: Section 1.2 defines the scope object as `exposure`, `domain`, `risk_signals`, and `stack`. Why this needs verification: This assumes those dimensions are enough to drive prompt specialization. The alternative is that critical distinctions like data criticality, multi-tenancy, deployment topology, regulatory scope, or online/offline workloads matter more than the chosen fields. Evidence required: Evidence from prior failures showing these exact fields predict missing adversary coverage, or prototype prompt-generation results across varied specs. Dependency risk: Dynamic prompting quality depends on the classification model being right.

**The spec assumes guardrails can detect contradictions by being told only "the changes are in sections [X, Y, Z]," but provides no proof section-delta awareness exists.** Claim: Section 4.1 prompt structure for CONS depends on knowing revised sections. Why this needs verification: This assumes the system tracks exact changed sections after each incorporation. The alternative is that revisions are conversational and not section-addressable, especially after large rewrites. Evidence required: Existing tooling or workflow that records changed sections per round, or a prototype showing Claude can derive them reliably. Dependency risk: Guardrail precision and token efficiency both depend on this.

**"A single Codex pass found 10 contradictions the entire pipeline missed" is used as strong evidence without methodology.** Claim: Overview / Evidence Base cites this as a key finding. Why this needs verification: This is a comparative performance claim about different review systems. The alternative is that Codex had access to different context, different spec version, or looser acceptance criteria. Evidence required: The exact contradiction list, evaluation method, and apples-to-apples comparison setup. Dependency risk: Much of the rationale for checkpoint guardrails and taxonomy emphasis leans on this evidence.

**The spec assumes the final gauntlet run log from `save_gauntlet_run()` is the "most reliable source of truth" without citing schema stability.** Claim: Section 3.2 says the final run log is the most reliable source for evaluations, verdicts, and full concern details. Why this needs verification: This is a code/data-contract claim. The alternative is that some needed data only exists in intermediate artifacts, or the run log schema varies across versions. Evidence required: Citation to `save_gauntlet_run()` output schema or a sample artifact from the current codebase. Dependency risk: `synthesis_extract.py` depends entirely on this source being complete and stable.

**The synthesis taxonomy itself is asserted as standard without evidence that it matches the actual concern distribution.** Claim: The spec repeatedly calls this the "standard 8-category taxonomy" and wants it enforced in code. Why this needs verification: This assumes eight categories are the correct abstraction and mutually useful. The alternative is that categories are leaky, overlapping, or too coarse for synthesis quality. Evidence required: Citation to the source of this standard, prior successful runs using it, or analysis showing it improves synthesis over ad hoc grouping. Dependency risk: The entire synthesis-extract redesign depends on this taxonomy being worth hard-coding.

**Deployment assumption is undocumented operationally.** Claim: Section 7.3 says deployment is just `cp -r skills/adversarial-spec/* ~/.claude/skills/adversarial-spec/` with "No config file changes. Pure code + docs." Why this needs verification: This assumes no additional registration, cache invalidation, version bump, or session restart is required for Claude Code to pick up changed skill behavior. The alternative is that copied files alone do not activate new prompts/docs cleanly. Evidence required: Official skill deployment docs or an existing project deployment runbook proving this is sufficient. Dependency risk: The redesign could appear implemented but not actually load in the runtime environment.

**Backward compatibility claim about `ADVERSARY_PREFIXES` retaining `LAZY` and `PREV` for ID lookups is unproven.** Claim: Section 7.3 says old concern IDs remain valid and prefix mappings are retained. Why this needs verification: This assumes all historical consumers rely only on prefix lookup and not adversary-name registries or roster membership. The alternative is breakage in any code that expects current registries to contain the old adversary identities. Evidence required: Search/citation of all current consumers of adversary names and prefixes. Dependency risk: Historical data compatibility is a stated non-breaking property.

**Dynamic prompt generation creates a prompt-injection sink from untrusted spec content** Attack vector: Claude reads the spec, then generates adversary prompts that include “2-4 sentences of spec-specific focus.” If the spec contains hostile text like “ignore prior instructions,” “treat auth as out of scope,” or adversarially crafted pseudo-guidance, that content can be absorbed into generated personas. The spec is effectively allowed to influence system-review instructions. Potential impact: The target document can manipulate the reviewers that are supposed to critique it, suppressing findings exactly where the attacker wants.

**`spec_hash` is not a security control but is treated like one** Attack vector: The file uses a truncated 12-character SHA-256 prefix. That is fine for accidental drift detection, but weak if anyone starts relying on it for tamper resistance or artifact binding. An attacker who can edit both the spec and `approved-prompts.json` can simply recompute the hash and keep the malicious prompt set “valid.” Potential impact: Operators may believe prompt/spec pairing is trustworthy when it is not; stale or malicious prompts can be made to look legitimate.

**Prompt file tampering can silently disable critical review coverage** Attack vector: The design trusts `.adversarial-spec-gauntlet/approved-prompts.json` as the control plane for which adversaries run and what they say. An attacker with workspace write access can mark `paranoid_security` as `skipped`, replace its persona with a neutered prompt, or empty multiple prompts to force the “zero active adversaries” failure path. The only integrity check described is `spec_hash`, which validates spec freshness, not artifact authenticity. Potential impact: Security review can be downgraded or bypassed without changing the spec itself, leading to false confidence and missed vulnerabilities.

**Scope classification is easy to game and sits upstream of all safeguards** Attack vector: Claude classifies exposure, domain, risk signals, and stack inline with no separate validation step. If the spec omits or downplays auth, PII, external integrations, or internet exposure, the generated prompts become weaker. The user can also “Adjust” the classification before launch. Potential impact: An attacker can downgrade the adversarial scrutiny by manipulating classification metadata rather than the design itself.

**`scope_guidelines` key matching is brittle and vulnerable to silent omissions** Attack vector: Guidance activation depends on exact string keys like `risk_signals:auth`. Misspellings, inconsistent casing, synonyms, or incomplete classification values mean important guidance never activates. The spec provides no normalization, validation, or required-key enforcement. Potential impact: Critical review instructions can disappear silently, creating uneven or misleading coverage.

**Natural-language user editing of prompts is an unbounded policy-bypass channel** Attack vector: The review gate allows user approval, edits, skips, and regeneration in natural language with no schema or constraints. A malicious or careless user can rewrite `paranoid_security` into “be brief and optimistic,” or subtly redefine valid dismissal criteria. Potential impact: The strongest reviewers can be socially engineered or intentionally declawed while preserving a normal-looking workflow.

**Static fallback personas become a shadow policy surface with no equivalent review gate** Attack vector: Even after investing in dynamic prompts, `generate_attacks()` can still fall back adversary-by-adversary if a key is missing from `prompts`. That means the executed persona may be an unreviewed static string from `ADVERSARIES`. Potential impact: Attackers can selectively drop only the most important dynamic prompts and rely on weaker, outdated, or inconsistent static text.

**The approved prompt artifact stores full reviewer personas in a writable location** Attack vector: `.adversarial-spec-gauntlet/approved-prompts.json` contains detailed `full_persona` strings, scope classification, risk signals, and stack info. That is effectively the system’s review strategy and threat-model configuration, sitting in plaintext beside the project. Potential impact: An attacker who reads the workspace learns exactly what reviewers will check, what they will skip, and how to craft designs that evade scrutiny.

**The design creates a reviewer-targeting map for adversarial evasion** Attack vector: The coverage matrix and per-adversary focus areas are explicit and standardized. A hostile spec author can distribute weaknesses across category boundaries, phrasing problems as “operability” instead of “security,” or “correctness” instead of “state machine invariants,” to exploit classifier and synthesis blind spots. Potential impact: Issues can be shaped to fall between adversaries or be down-ranked during synthesis.

**Concern text is copied into synthesis input with no sanitization policy** Attack vector: The output format includes raw concern text with newlines replaced by spaces, but no escaping, filtering, or truncation beyond 150 chars. If concern text contains prompt-manipulation strings, terminal control sequences, deceptive bracket patterns, or metadata-looking text, it can poison downstream synthesis. Potential impact: The one-pass synthesis step can be steered, confused, or made to misclassify findings.

**`synthesis_extract.py` can leak sensitive content into flat text artifacts** Attack vector: The extraction output includes the first 150 characters of every concern text and prepends a synthesis header, writing to `.adversarial-spec-gauntlet/synthesis-input-HASH.txt`. If concerns mention secrets, endpoint details, internal architecture, or exploit narratives, they are duplicated into another plaintext file. Potential impact: Sensitive review findings are spread into additional artifacts, increasing exposure and persistence.

**`consistency_auditor` is a single point of failure for contradiction detection** Attack vector: CONS failure is a hard stop, but the detection itself depends on one cheap model call over a 5K-20K token spec. Long specs, subtle contradictions, and prompt injection from the spec can all reduce reliability. There is no secondary validation, deterministic checker, or diff-aware structural analysis. Potential impact: High-confidence contradictions can still slip through, yet the process may treat a clean CONS run as strong evidence of consistency.

**The spec over-trusts Claude as both classifier, prompt author, reviewer, and orchestrator** Attack vector: Claude classifies scope, generates prompts, mediates user edits, runs guardrails inline, and handles fix incorporation logic. That is extreme concentration of authority with no independent verifier or separation of duties. Potential impact: One compromised or confused agent can corrupt the entire review pipeline end to end.

**No authentication or authorization boundary is defined for who can approve prompt changes** Attack vector: The spec repeatedly says “the user reviews” and “user approves/edits/skips,” but it never defines who counts as the authorized approver in shared environments, pair sessions, CI-triggered runs, or delegated agents. Potential impact: Any actor with access to the session could weaken review policy, and later claim the prompts were “user-approved.”

**The spec explicitly avoids changing `Concern` and `Evaluation` models, which may lock in unsafe assumptions** Attack vector: The redesign adds dynamic prompts, skipping, guardrails, and taxonomy enforcement, but refuses to evolve the core data models. That means there is no structured place for provenance like “which prompt version generated this concern,” “was this adversary skipped,” or “which artifact approved this prompt.” Potential impact: Auditability is weak. After an incident, you may not be able to prove what reviewer logic actually ran.

**The new `traffic_engineer` can be skipped or diluted even for public systems with DoS risk** Attack vector: Scalability is treated as coverage optimization, not a mandatory security control. Many denial-of-service and resource exhaustion flaws will present as throughput or fan-out issues rather than classic “security” bugs. Potential impact: Public-facing systems can ship with exploitable exhaustion paths because performance review is optional or deprioritized.

**The system records skip reasons in plaintext, which can reveal attack intent or reviewer manipulation** Attack vector: `approved-prompts.json` stores human-readable `skip_reason`. In a hostile or audited environment, those reasons can leak rationales like “security review skipped because internal refactor only,” giving attackers confidence about which lanes are unwatched. Potential impact: Adversaries gain operational intelligence about review blind spots and organizational risk tolerance.

**No retention or cleanup policy exists for generated review artifacts** Attack vector: `approved-prompts.json`, gauntlet run logs, and synthesis-input files accumulate under `.adversarial-spec-gauntlet/`. Those artifacts contain scope, stack, risk signals, findings, and review strategy. Potential impact: Long-lived local artifacts increase exposure to later compromise, accidental commit, cross-project leakage, or insider snooping.

Dynamic prompts are generated conversationally by Claude, but execution happens later in Python via a file artifact, and the handoff contract is under-specified. The user flow: user approves prompts in chat, Claude writes `.adversarial-spec-gauntlet/approved-prompts.json`, later `run_gauntlet()` loads it if present and silently falls back on mismatch/missing. There is no lifecycle state saying whether this spec revision is “armed”, invalidated, or partially approved. The impact is stale-state bugs: the user thinks they approved a scoped roster, but a later spec edit quietly reverts to static personas and the gauntlet runs with materially different behavior. Structural alternative: persist an explicit prompt bundle state machine with statuses like `draft`, `approved_for_spec_hash`, `invalidated`, `consumed`; make `run_gauntlet()` require an explicit mode (`strict approved prompts` vs `allow fallback`) instead of always guessing.

The spec claims prompt generation is “not a separate pipeline,” but it introduces a new multi-step sub-system with classification, review, artifact persistence, skip decisions, and execution-time validation. The user flow: before gauntlet, Claude classifies scope, presents prompts, user edits/skips, Claude writes JSON, Python later validates hash and roster. That is a separate pipeline in practice, just hidden in prose. The downstream impact is ownership confusion: failures in classification, review, artifact write, hash invalidation, and fallback do not belong cleanly to either the conversational layer or the runtime. Structural alternative: define a formal “adversary arming” phase with inputs, outputs, and failure semantics, even if it is still performed conversationally.

`run_gauntlet()` is being turned into an orchestration choke point for artifact loading, hash validation, roster filtering, prompt flattening, and error policy, while `generate_attacks()` remains deliberately dumb. The user flow: everything meaningful about dynamic prompting happens in `run_gauntlet()`, and `generate_attacks()` just consumes a pre-filtered list plus optional prompt dict. The impact is a leaky boundary: every future caller must remember the exact preconditions, or skipped adversaries run via static fallback. This is already acknowledged by the spec’s own “skip-filtering safety test,” which is a sign the abstraction is wrong. Structural alternative: define a single `PreparedGauntletRun` object returned by a preparation step and accepted by execution; `generate_attacks()` should consume structured adversary configs, not parallel primitive lists that must stay in sync.

`FLOW: approved-prompts.json -> run_gauntlet() (prompt overrides + skip decisions)` Mechanism: Explicit file read. Source capabilities: Local JSON artifact. Latency requirement: None specified. Assessment: FLAG. §1.7 says `run_gauntlet()` loads, validates hash, filters adversaries, and passes prompts onward, but the schema contract is inconsistent with the stated fallback behavior. If the file contains some approved prompts and some missing entries, `generate_attacks()` falls back per-adversary to static personas. That silently mixes reviewed dynamic prompts with unreviewed static prompts. The spec never says whether partial approval is allowed, whether missing keys are an error, or whether the user must explicitly approve the final active roster.

The entire redesign is justified by failures in editorial contradiction detection, yet the core architectural change invests heavily in better gauntlet prompting and roster tuning rather than formalizing revision-aware consistency mechanisms. The user flow: the system still relies on LLM-based guardrails to catch contradictions introduced by human/LLM edits after each round; nothing structural prevents summary/detail divergence or invariant breakage. The downstream impact is tech debt disguised as process: you are optimizing reviewers instead of reducing contradiction creation. Structural alternative: add structured spec invariants, requirement trace maps, and cross-reference indexes that can be machine-checked before invoking LLM reviewers.

The design assumes Claude can classify scope, generate prompts, review edits, run guardrails, and manage spec revisions “inline,” but there is no shared infrastructure for caching or invalidating these expensive intermediate judgments as the spec changes. The user flow: after each substantive revision, the user may need new guardrails; before gauntlet, prompts must be regenerated if the spec meaningfully changed; there is no normalized diffing or dependency tracking. The impact is redundant work and state drift, especially across long debates. Structural alternative: introduce revision-aware metadata keyed by spec hash and section diffs so only affected guardrails/prompts are invalidated.

`FLOW: Spec revision -> spec_hash -> stale prompt detection` Mechanism: Explicit hash comparison. Source capabilities: Raw spec file contents hashed by SHA-256 truncated to 12 chars. Latency requirement: None specified. Assessment: FLAG. §1.6/§1.7 assumes a single canonical “raw spec file,” but the spec is about a conversational Claude workflow and repeatedly references section-level incorporation during debate. It never specifies which file is hashed when the spec may be in-memory, unsaved, or edited incrementally. The stale-prompt guardrail only works if the exact same source-of-truth file is consistently used on both write and read paths; that wiring is missing.

`FLOW: scope_guidelines selection -> assembled persona` Mechanism: Implicit rule engine inside Claude. Source capabilities: Template dict keys like `risk_signals:auth`. Latency requirement: None specified. Assessment: FLAG. The assembly mechanism is not actually specified. §1.3 says Claude selects relevant guidelines and generates “2-4 sentences of spec-specific focus,” but there is no deterministic construction rule, token budget, ordering, conflict resolution, or guarantee that fixed evaluation rules remain semantically intact once wrapped in generated prose. You say fixed fields are “never changed,” but the effective prompt still changes by composition.

`FLOW: approved prompts -> generate_attacks() (persona text)` Mechanism: Explicit function parameter `prompts: dict[str, str]`. Source capabilities: Flat dict extracted from artifact. Latency requirement: None specified. Assessment: FLAG. §1.7 says no changes to system prompt template construction, yet you are now injecting fully assembled personas from outside `ADVERSARIES`. The spec does not define escaping, sanitization, or max length. A user-edited prompt can alter structure, break assumptions in downstream prompt assembly, or balloon token cost. This is an external-input-to-system-prompt flow with no guardrails.

`FLOW: guardrail model failure -> proceeding behavior` Mechanism: Error policy table in §5. Source capabilities: Cheap/free model call or Claude inline. Latency requirement: Guardrails run after every revision. Assessment: FLAG. The failure policy is inconsistent with the process semantics. CONS failing is a hard stop; SCOPE/TRACE failing is a “soft stop” requiring explicit user approval. But §4.2 made all three part of the mandatory checkpoint gate. The flow from model-call result to workflow continuation is not coherently specified.

`FLOW: gauntlet run log -> synthesis_extract.py -> synthesis-input-HASH.txt` Mechanism: Explicit CLI file transformation. Source capabilities: Final run log from `save_gauntlet_run()`. Latency requirement: None specified. Assessment: FLAG. §3.2 says the extractor outputs one line per concern and prepends a synthesis prompt header. That means one artifact mixes machine-extracted records with instruction prose. The downstream consumer is Claude, but the spec never says whether the file is intended as a human-readable prompt, a machine-readable intermediary, or both. You are collapsing data and instructions into a single unlabeled channel.

`FLOW: guardrail outputs -> no formal Concern objects` Mechanism: Lightweight inline text only. Source capabilities: Claude-generated findings. Latency requirement: After every revision. Assessment: FLAG. §4.4 explicitly avoids formal IDs and structured objects. That means there is no durable identifier for a contradiction found in round N, no state linkage to its resolution, and no way to audit whether a finding reappears later. The system is trying to prevent compounding regressions while discarding the structure needed to track them.

`FLOW: pipeline verdicts in run log -> synthesis judgment` Mechanism: Explicit inclusion of `verdict={pipeline_verdict}` in each line. Source capabilities: Prior evaluation result. Latency requirement: None specified. Assessment: FLAG. §3.2/§3.3 says include pipeline verdicts but “do NOT pre-filter” and have Claude re-evaluate all concerns. This is a biasing information flow. You are feeding prior adjudication labels into the synthesis prompt and pretending it will not anchor the final categorization and verdicting. If independent synthesis is the goal, this flow is contaminating it.

`FLOW: all concerns regardless of verdict -> one-pass synthesis` Mechanism: Explicit inclusion. Source capabilities: Full run log. Latency requirement: None specified. Assessment: FLAG. No mechanism is specified for controlling prompt size. A large gauntlet run can exceed context once you prepend instructions and include every concern. The spec mandates one-pass synthesis but provides no truncation, chunking, or overflow behavior. That is a broken data transport plan.

`FLOW: aliases -> canonical names -> prefix lookup` Mechanism: Explicit resolution function. Source capabilities: `ADVERSARY_ALIASES`. Latency requirement: None specified. Assessment: FLAG. §7.2 says the new resolution layer replaces dict aliases because dict aliasing “doesn't preserve prefix lookup,” but §7.3 also says `ADVERSARY_PREFIXES` retains `LAZY` and `PREV` for ID lookups while new runs use `MINI`. The mapping between name resolution and prefix resolution is not fully specified. Historical IDs, new prefixes, and canonical names now travel on separate tracks.

`FLOW: external boundary .docx evidence file -> spec conclusions` Mechanism: Reference only, no parsing path specified. Source capabilities: `extended commentary on adversaries.docx`. Latency requirement: None specified. Assessment: FLAG. The evidence base includes a binary `.docx` artifact, but there is no mechanism for how implementers or Claude consume it. If important adversary-to-taxonomy mapping lives there, the information source is effectively opaque and non-automatable.

`FLOW: “information_flow_auditor” remains unchanged despite this redesign targeting missed editorial/dataflow issues` Mechanism: Existing adversary in roster. Source capabilities: Unknown from this spec. Latency requirement: None specified. Assessment: FLAG. The spec’s own failure mode is unspecified/implicit information movement, yet §2.5 leaves `information_flow_auditor` “unchanged.” No updated brief requires it to challenge unlabeled arrows, push-vs-poll decisions, file artifact contracts, or session-to-runtime handoffs introduced elsewhere in this redesign. The roster changes add traffic coverage but do not clearly add control-plane flow coverage where the redesign is weakest.

`FLOW: user approval for scope additions -> persistent approval memory` Mechanism: Conversational approval in §4.2/§4.3. Source capabilities: User response. Latency requirement: Before proceeding. Assessment: FLAG. The spec says SCOPE should allow “approved” additions, but it never defines where those approvals are stored so future checkpoints stop flagging the same scope. Without a persisted approval ledger, the detector either repeats findings forever or relies on Claude session memory.

Truncating SHA-256 to 12 hex characters for `spec_hash` creates a non-zero collision risk. In production, many runs across many specs and revisions accumulate over time, especially if checkpoints are retained. The consequence is stale prompt approval being incorrectly accepted for a different spec version.

The fallback behavior is underspecified for partial corruption. If `approved-prompts.json` is syntactically valid JSON but semantically incomplete, some adversaries may have `approved`, some `skipped`, and some missing. In production, a crash or manual edit can easily produce this state. The consequence is ambiguous roster construction, mixed static/dynamic behavior, and nondeterministic attack coverage.

“User edits prompt to empty string → Treat as skip” does not define what counts as empty. In production, a user may submit whitespace, Markdown comments, zero-width Unicode, or a prompt containing only formatting. The consequence is inconsistent skip detection and hard-to-debug roster differences.

The design makes skipping “always a user decision,” but later says `run_gauntlet()` filters by `status: "skipped"` from persisted state. In production, stale state from an earlier review can still skip adversaries if the user never explicitly re-approved after a spec edit. The consequence is hidden persistence of old human decisions across materially changed scopes.

The zero-active-adversaries case hard-stops with `ValueError`, but the UX around how a user recovers is undefined. In production, a user can intentionally skip everything while testing the feature. The consequence is a hard crash path in a conversational workflow with no prescribed remediation.

CONS is defined as a static-prompt adversary even though it explicitly depends on “the changes are in sections [X, Y, Z].” In production, either Claude must manually inject dynamic change context into a supposedly static prompt, or the prompt is too vague. The consequence is either contradiction between the design and implementation model, or degraded consistency checking.

The spec says guardrails do not run on the first draft because there is no previous version to compare against, but SCOPE and TRACE are defined against original requirements and roadmap, not previous revisions. In production, they could be useful on the first draft but are disabled by policy. The consequence is early scope creep and orphaned requirements going uncaught.

The PEDA vs ASSH split into data-level vs design-level correctness is cleaner on paper than in real defects. In production, many bugs span both, such as schema evolution breaking invariants or encoding bugs violating API contracts. The consequence is duplicated findings, conflicting dismissals, or reviewer confusion over ownership.

The spec says no changes to `Concern` or `Evaluation` data models, but also changes adversary names, prefixes, and taxonomy expectations. In production, downstream analytics, dashboards, or filtering code may implicitly assume old adversary sets or category mappings. The consequence is silent reporting breakage without a schema migration.

The extraction script outputs `text_first_150_chars`, which can cut through Unicode grapheme clusters, surrogate pairs, or combining marks if implemented naïvely. In production, specs and concerns can include non-ASCII text. The consequence is malformed output, unreadable summaries, or even downstream parser issues.

Replacing newlines in concern text with spaces is underspecified for tabs, repeated whitespace, code fences, or extremely long unbroken tokens. In production, concern text may contain stack traces, code snippets, or URLs. The consequence is either unreadable synthesis input or lines that exceed practical limits for model ingestion and tooling.

The synthesis header tells Claude to assign exactly one primary category and a verdict of `accept/acknowledge/dismiss`, while the run log contains `pipeline_verdict` values like `accepted` and `deferred`. In production, humans and models will conflate pipeline verdict and synthesis verdict. The consequence is semantic drift where “deferred” concerns get force-fit into unrelated verdict labels.

“Exit 0 for empty, exit 2 for invalid schema” leaves other failure modes undefined, such as unreadable file, permissions error, truncated JSON, or output path collision. In production, these happen routinely. The consequence is inconsistent automation behavior and poor debuggability.

The dynamic prompt system depends on a user review before gauntlet launch, but the fallback path allows gauntlet execution with static personas whenever the artifact is missing. In production, any failure in the review UX effectively bypasses the new safety/quality mechanism. The consequence is partial rollout where teams believe they are getting reviewed dynamic prompts but often are not.

`generated_at` is recorded in UTC but the workflow examples and environment are local-time driven. In production, users debugging recency or stale-prompt behavior will compare local file edits to UTC artifact timestamps. The consequence is operator confusion and mistaken stale/fresh decisions.

The finalize phase runs CONS across the entire spec, but the checks include “Every file in implementation plans matches the module structure in architecture sections.” In production, implementation plans often intentionally evolve later than high-level architecture. The consequence is false-positive contradictions for planned-but-not-finalized module names.

`resolve_adversary_name()` returns unknown names unchanged, while the error table says unknown names should be skipped with warning. In production, unchanged unknown names can flow deeper into code before failing lookup. The consequence is inconsistent error handling depending on call site order.

The dual registry model says `ADVERSARIES` “also contains aliases for backward compatibility,” but later says aliasing is replaced by explicit `ADVERSARY_ALIASES` because dict aliasing does not preserve prefix lookup. In production, that is contradictory design guidance. The consequence is implementers may redundantly add both and create diverging sources of truth.

The backward compatibility section updates `ADVERSARY_PREFIXES` to retain `LAZY` and `PREV` for ID lookups, but there is no definition of behavior when a legacy concern is re-evaluated or displayed alongside `MINI`. In production, reports and synthesis may mix categories for semantically related adversaries. The consequence is confusing analytics and inconsistent grouping.

The testing strategy omits adversarial tests for malformed Unicode, huge prompt files, extremely large concern texts, or very large adversary lists. In production, those are classic edge conditions for JSON artifacts and CLI text generation. The consequence is a redesign that passes happy-path tests but fails on boundary conditions.

The “special phases unchanged” note exempts `existing_system_compatibility` and `ux_architect`, but nothing says whether they participate in taxonomy coverage, prompt review, or approved prompt artifacts. In production, these out-of-band adversaries become second-class citizens with undefined behavior. The consequence is inconsistent user mental models and incomplete synthesis inputs.

The spec assumes a single `.adversarial-spec-gauntlet` directory for all artifact lifecycles, but does not define cleanup, retention, or namespacing for multiple branches/specs. In production, artifact buildup and hash collisions across branches are inevitable. The consequence is stale-file ambiguity and accidental reuse.

The prompt examples and output formats rely on section references like `§5.1` and `§8.4`, but the spec does not require normalized section numbering in all target specs. In production, many specs have headings without section numbers. The consequence is guardrail output becoming less actionable or depending on brittle inferred references.

The CONS check includes arithmetic consistency like `5s + 3s != 5s`, but latency budgets and retries often involve percentiles, overlaps, or parallel branches. In production, simplistic arithmetic checks will misfire on non-additive budgets. The consequence is false contradictions distracting from real ones.

The TRACE check requires that “test cases still map to spec sections,” but the redesign is for a spec-development skill and not every spec has explicit test cases at all stages. In production, early drafts or architecture-only specs will fail this expectation. The consequence is noisy findings from an assumed artifact that may not exist.

The design depends heavily on Claude doing orchestration, classification, prompt generation, prompt review, guardrail execution, and synthesis handoff, but the spec simultaneously tries to enforce behavior in code. In production, any session-context loss or model variation changes the non-coded parts dramatically. The consequence is a system whose most critical behavior remains procedural and hard to verify despite the stated goal of guardrails and enforcement.

**Failure mode: “skip adversary” is framed as user review ergonomics, but it is an availability risk to coverage.** Sections 1.5 and 1.7 allow skipping adversaries and only hard-stop at zero remaining. There is no minimum coverage policy by taxonomy, no warning if the user skips the only primary reviewer for Security or Scalability, and no enforcement against gutting the roster. **How operators find out:** they won’t, until an outage or missed class of bug shows up later. **Blast radius:** entire defect classes become undetectable while the system still reports a successful gauntlet run.

**Failure mode: `traffic_engineer` is added as a cheap conceptual fix for scalability, but there is no evidence the underlying models can reason about throughput credibly from prose alone.** Section 2.2 piles on load concepts like hot keys, pool exhaustion, and herd effects, but the spec doesn’t define expected traffic inputs, SLOs, or system bounds. This adversary may generate plausible-sounding nonsense. **How operators find out:** only when recommendations don’t map to real capacity data, or when teams waste time chasing invented bottlenecks. **Blast radius:** noisy false positives in one of the hardest domains to validate.

**Failure mode: the finalize phase only reruns CONS, not SCOPE or TRACE, so last-minute scope drift and orphaned requirements can slip through final edits.** Section 4.6 narrows final quality check to consistency only. If the final incorporation adds or removes feature content cleanly, CONS will pass while requirements coverage and non-goal integrity are broken. **How operators find out:** only after implementation starts or a stakeholder notices missing scope alignment. **Blast radius:** final spec can be internally consistent and still wrong.

**Failure mode: the redesign adds multiple new sources of truth without a coherence model.** You now have the spec file, approved prompts artifact, run log, synthesis input file, session file, roadmap manifest, static adversary registry, template registry, and phase docs. The spec never states which one wins when they disagree. **How operators find out:** through contradictory outputs and hard-to-reproduce runs. **Blast radius:** cross-phase integrity degrades; debugging any anomaly requires archaeology across half a dozen artifacts.

**Failure mode: the system optimizes for user convenience over fail-closed behavior in several dangerous places.** Natural-language prompt editing, optional skips, conversational approval, soft-stops for SCOPE/TRACE, exit 0 on empty extraction. This is a reviewer-assurance system that keeps choosing permissive defaults. **How operators find out:** after a missed requirement, contradiction, or outage-causing flaw slips through. **Blast radius:** broad; the whole redesign can report success while materially reducing scrutiny.

**Failure mode: there is no explicit metric proving the redesign improved recall on the editorial regressions it targets.** The spec cites failure reports and proposes mechanisms, but there is no acceptance criterion like “CONS must catch the 10 contradictions from the BracketBattleAI report” or “guardrails must block regression reintroduction in replay.” **How operators find out:** they can’t know whether the redesign works until it fails again in live use. **Blast radius:** you may spend substantial implementation effort adding complexity without actually closing the failure mode that paged everyone in the first place.

**Failure mode: no provider outage strategy exists for guardrails or dynamic prompt generation.** The whole redesign assumes Claude and cheap models are available inline. There is no local fallback, cached result policy, offline mode, queueing behavior, or resumability. **How operators find out:** at the worst time, when the model provider is degraded and the workflow hard-stops in mid-debate. **Blast radius:** authoring pipeline outage; users cannot progress or must bypass safeguards manually.

**You’re reimplementing traceability as a bespoke adversary instead of using a requirements matrix artifact.** Quote: `requirements_tracer` checks that “every user story from the roadmap still has coverage.” Simpler alternative: Maintain a simple requirements-to-section mapping table and validate it during revision. Why that would work: Traceability is a classic matrix problem. A maintained mapping is auditable and deterministic. An LLM “QA lead” pass is fuzzy and expensive. What requirement breaks if we use the simpler approach: The requirement to infer coverage semantically without maintaining another artifact. If you refuse an artifact, prove that the LLM’s inference is reliable enough to replace one.

**You claim “no changes to downstream phases,” but the new prompts can materially change concern distribution and synthesis behavior.** Quote: “No changes to… concern parsing, or any downstream phases.” Simpler alternative: Treat dynamic prompts as a behavioral pipeline change and update downstream evaluation guidance accordingly. Why that would work: If prompts become scope-aware and more aggressive, acceptance rates, category balance, and synthesis inputs will change. Pretending downstream phases are unaffected is wishful thinking. What requirement breaks if we use the simpler approach: More implementation work. But behavioral changes without downstream recalibration are how you create new blind spots.

**You are adding implementation instructions into the spec body in a way that will rot immediately.** Quote: “For a developer implementing this spec: 1. Read `scripts/adversaries.py`… 8. Test with a real debate + gauntlet run.” Simpler alternative: Put implementation sequencing in a task card or dev plan, not in the technical design spec. Why that would work: Design specs should describe target behavior. Step-by-step file-edit order is execution planning and will go stale as soon as the code moves. What requirement breaks if we use the simpler approach: The requirement to make implementation easy to pick up. That belongs in a work plan, not mixed into the design contract.

**The spec introduces “prompt review” and “prompt regeneration” but never defines acceptance criteria for a good generated prompt.** Quote: user can approve, edit, skip, regenerate; templates have tone/focus/rules. Simpler alternative: Define explicit prompt quality checks: must preserve fixed dismissal rules, must include only activated scope guidance, must stay under N tokens, must not duplicate another adversary’s focus. Why that would work: Without prompt quality criteria, review is subjective and inconsistent. The whole dynamic-prompt subsystem depends on generated prompts being good. What requirement breaks if we use the simpler approach: Additional validation complexity. But if you don’t validate prompt quality, you’re trusting the most failure-prone part of the redesign.

**The claim that guardrails are for “editorial regressions” is too narrow for what the prompts actually ask them to do.** Quote: CONS checks scope boundaries, function names, numeric values, phase descriptions, docs, etc.; SCOPE checks project drift; TRACE checks acceptance criteria coverage. Simpler alternative: Split editorial consistency from scope/governance checks and treat them as different workflows. Why that would work: Contradictions, unauthorized scope growth, and requirement traceability are different classes of failure with different evidence and remediation. Bundling them under “checkpoint guardrails” makes the process heavier and muddier. What requirement breaks if we use the simpler approach: The requirement for one compact post-revision check step. Convenience is not a great reason to conflate different controls.

**LLM Hashing Hallucination (Section 1.6)** You are expecting Claude to natively generate `approved-prompts.json` and include a `spec_hash` defined as the "SHA-256 hex digest of the raw spec file contents". LLMs cannot compute cryptographic hashes of large text files. Claude will hallucinate a 12-character hex string. When `orchestrator.py` actually runs and computes the real hash, it will mismatch every single time. Your entire dynamic prompt caching mechanism is dead on arrival.

**Alias Resolution Blindspot (Section 7.2)** Failure Mode:** `resolve_adversary_name()` is added to map `lazy_developer` to `minimalist`. But if an old `approved-prompts.json` has the key `lazy_developer`, and `run_gauntlet` loads it into the `prompts` dict, looking up `minimalist` in that dict will yield a KeyError or `None` unless the keys in the JSON are *also* passed through the resolver upon load. Operator Awareness:** Silent fallback to static personas despite a valid cached prompt existing. Blast Radius:** Broken backward compatibility across system upgrades.

**Format Regression in Extraction (Section 3.2)** You are taking clean, structured data from `run-TIMESTAMP-HASH.json` and using a python script to convert it into a bespoke, pipe-delimited plaintext format (`[{id}] {adversary} | {severity} | verdict=...`) just to feed it *back* to an LLM. LLMs natively understand JSON. You are introducing a flaky custom parsing format for absolutely no reason.

**Duplicate Execution via Aliasing (Section 7.2)** Edge case:** A user's configuration specifies `adversaries: ["lazy_developer", "minimalist"]` because they were in the middle of migrating their config and left both. Production occurrence:** `resolve_adversary_name()` turns this list into `["minimalist", "minimalist"]`. Consequence:** The spec does not state that `generate_attacks()` or `run_gauntlet()` deduplicates the list. `minimalist` will execute twice, doubling the API cost and flooding the synthesis phase with duplicate concerns.

**Infinite Guardrail Loops (Section 4.2)** Edge case:** `consistency_auditor` (CONS) flags a contradiction. Claude autonomously tries to fix it during the debate phase. Production occurrence:** Claude's fix resolves the first contradiction but inadvertently creates a new one, or violates a constraint caught by `scope_creep_detector` (SCOPE). Consequence:** Step 2a mandates "Fix contradictions (CONS) before proceeding" as a blocking step. Claude could loop forever trying to satisfy the guardrails, burning through the token budget and hitting rate limits (which your evidence base already notes is a problem). There is no circuit breaker defined for guardrail failure.

The spec introduces two tiers of adversaries but leaves them on incompatible abstractions: guardrails use static `Adversary`, gauntlet uses `AdversaryTemplate`, both are “adversaries,” neither shares a common execution contract. The user flow: an implementer adds a new reviewer and must decide whether it belongs in `GUARDRAILS`, `ADVERSARIES`, `ADVERSARY_TEMPLATES`, or multiple places, each with different semantics. The impact is pattern propagation risk: the first implementation locks in a bifurcated mental model and every future addition becomes a bespoke routing decision. Structural alternative: introduce a common reviewer interface with phase eligibility, prompt strategy, output type, and required inputs; then guardrails and gauntlet reviewers are configuration variants, not separate species.

The migration plan puts both `minimalist` and `traffic_engineer` in both `ADVERSARIES` and `ADVERSARY_TEMPLATES`, while also saying `ADVERSARIES` contains aliases for backward compatibility. The user flow: code iterates `ADVERSARIES` for default roster, serialization, docs, or help output and accidentally includes aliases as if they were real adversaries, or counts them twice. The impact is polluted runtime semantics from using a lookup registry as a domain model. Structural alternative: keep canonical adversaries in one collection and aliases in a separate resolver only; never represent aliases as full runtime entries.
